items.title: FileCompare makes data differences crystal clear
items.author: zhouhua
items.content:
What Is Compare Two Lists?

A text comparison tool named Compare Two Lists appears to be very powerful in that it analyzes and compares the two separate lists of items to find the differences between them, the similarities, and the unique items. This utility makes it possible for users to put in two separate sets of data and then decide fast which items are in both lists, which are only in one of the lists, and the complete list of all the unique items.

The feature is especially useful in the areas of data analysis, inventory control, configuration comparison, and content review. No matter if you're dealing with product catalogs, customer lists, file variations, or duplicates in entries, this tool provides instant visual feedback showing the connections between your datasets.

The function allows users to discern differences between similar datasets quickly and thus brings an indispensable utility to the quality assurance, data validation, and change tracking processes. This solution is fully browser-based and involves no server communication, thus guaranteeing that your data is completely private and secure.

How To Use Compare Two Lists

The use of the tool is very easy and straightforward. First, input your first list of items into the "List A" text area, making sure each item is on a different line. Next, put the second list in the "List B" text area, still following the one-item-per-line rule.

Various ways to customize your comparison are offered by the interface:

  • Enable or disable case sensitivity for rigid or flexible matching
  • Select whether to consider or ignore empty lines or whitespaces
  • For giving a more accurate comparison eliminate special characters
  • To make the text formatting uniform apply normalization
  • Decide on the sorting of results (ascending, descending, or none)

When your lists have been submitted and the settings have been adjusted, hit the "Compare Lists" button. Four different divisions will display the results: List A containing only its unique items, Items common to both lists, Items unique to List B, and the combined union of both lists. Each division may be filtered, sorted, or exported.

You can get data from text files, CSV files, or even JSON files directly into each list. The results are enhanced with colors indicating the different categories of items, and the statistics also reveal how many items there are in each comparison category.

FAQs (Frequently Asked Questions)

What is list comparison used for?

By means of list comparison, the user can spot differences and similarities between two datasets. This tool operates in such a way that the user gets to identify unique entries in the lists, get to know the common items between both lists, and also get the whole collection of unique items at once. Common examples of usage include inventory list comparison, customers' databases analysis, duplicate entries checking, configuration files change detection, and shopping lists comparison. Thus, the function becomes indispensable in cases where data analysis, quality control, and content management tasks have to be performed since it is very critical to know the relationship between datasets.

How does the list diff feature work?

The feature works by first analyzing two lists of text items separately and then identifying the relationships between them. When you provide List A and List B, the tool applies the options you have chosen (for example, case sensitivity, whitespace handling) to each item and then divides the results into four groups according to their uniqueness. Items only found in List A are displayed in one section, items common in both lists in the next one, items only in List B in the third one, and the union of all unique items in the last one. This functionality gives you instant visual feedback on your datasets' differences and similarities.

Can I import data into the compare list a and b tool?

The answer is yes, you can using the tool. The interface has import buttons that let you directly upload text files, CSV files, or JSON files to either List A or List B. When a JSON file is being imported, the tool automatically changes the data in the array to line-separated entries. CSV files are imported as plain text. This function lets you work with pre-existing data without the need for tedious manual copying and pasting. In addition, the import function helps maintain the integrity of your data while getting it ready for comparison operations.

Is my data secure when using this list compare tool?

100% your data is protected along with the usage of this tool. The whole process is done on your machine browser without any server interactions and data transfers at all. The feature works only on your computer so your confidential info could never be sent out from your machine. There is no data that is kept, recorded, or transactions made between you and third parties. The tool ensures that your privacy is protected to its fullest extent while at the same time offering you great comparison capabilities. Besides, the local processing guarantees fast results with no network delays.

What are the common use cases for compare 2 lists functionality?

The functionality is very useful and it is being used in different industries. Some of the common use cases are comparing product catalogs to find out which items are new or discontinued, analyzing customer lists to see how different segments overlap, checking for duplicate entries in databases, comparing software configuration files to find out what has been changed and auditing content between different document versions. The capability is very helpful in inventory management, data cleaning, quality assurance processes, and research analysis. This tool saves professionals’ time in manual comparison tasks.

How accurate is the list diff comparison?

The comparison is very accurate and delivers results depending on the comparison options that you have selected. The tool handles each item according to your settings for case sensitivity, whitespace handling, special character treatment, and normalization. Items are compared through exact string matching unless altered by your selected options. The algorithm preserves order when possible and systematically removes duplicates. For accuracy's sake, be sure that your data is properly formatted with only one item per line. The results not only provide the differences but also include statistics to verify the completeness and accuracy of the comparison.

items.title: Crystal 1.19.0 Is Released
items.author: Kerrick
items.content:
We are announcing a new Crystal release 1.19.0 with several new features and bug fixes.

This release includes 237 changes since 1.18.2 by 24 contributors. We thank all the contributors for all the effort put into improving the language! ❤️

Below we list the most remarkable changes in the language, compiler and stdlib. For more details, visit the full changelog.

We do not expect any breaking changes in existing code. If you notice any unexpected issues, please let us know in the issue tracker or forum.

The following changes break prior behavior of the compiler, but we expect them to not have much effect in existing code. If you notice any unexpected issues, please let us know in the issue tracker or forum.

The macro is now aware of name-value mappings in the form of where will now return the respective as a (#16310). A key without a value returns as implicit value, as before.

Instead of returning a the macro might now return a which might be unexpected. The value is still truthy and shouldn’t cause issues in practice.

For example, the value printed by `` may now be:

  • =>

The monotonic clock has been adjusted on Linux and macOS to include suspended time, when they previously counted the system uptime.

UNIX targets still use except for Linux that now uses (#16516) and macOS that now uses instead of that internally used (#16516, #16492).

The change is affecting for example, that should now include suspended time across the different targets, rather than sometimes including it, and sometimes not.

Execution contexts from RFC 0002 continue as a final preview feature with opt-in with compiler flags -Dpreview_mt -Dexecution_context and we plan to finally enable the feature by default in the 1.20 release!

There has not been huge developments to the feature, but corrections to fix the remaining thread safety issues in the standard library, for example:

  • Add thread safety to default random (#16174)
  • Closing system fd is thread unsafe (#16289)
  • Thread safety of (#16504)

We started to merge synchronization primitives from into the standard library as well:

Introduces the type to represent instants on the monotonic timeline as defined in RFC 0015 instead of overloading the type that represents a duration, not an instant (#16490). We expect a clearer and safer API:

  • represents a single point on the monotonic timeline.
  • continues to represent a duration.
  • Subtracting two instances yields a .
  • Adding/subtracting a to/from an yields a new .

We also adjusted the monotonic clock (#16516), applied the changes (#16498) and deprecated (#16545).

In order to migrate existing code to , replace calls to with . If the instant time reading is stored in an explicitly typed variable (e.g. an instance variable), change the type from to . The APIs of these types are very similar so most use cases should not need any further adaptions.

Finally, we made some further adjustments:

  • Treat GMT as a legacy alias of UTC (#16292)
  • Add to zoneinfo lookup paths (#16463)

A long refactor of spawning sub-processes has started. It encompasses a tedious cleanup and safety fixes related to finding the command as well as building the arguments and environment variables, to eventually move on from the classic then to the more modern on UNIX targets.

and now look up the target executable in the environment of the parent process () instead of in the environment of the child process () on all platforms (#16340). This is usually the expected and specified behaviour for spawning a new process, but it only worked correcly on Windows until now. Consider using a full path (possibly resolved via ) if you want to look up the executable in a different path. With , the target executable is also looked up in because the shell process sits in between.

  • Move before (#16351)
  • Replace with a non-raising alternative in pre-exec (#16352, #16369)
  • Fix reset directory if fails (#16383)
  • Disable process cancellation during (#16446)
  • Support large JSON files (#16211)
  • Support deserialization of YAML anchors of value types (#16186)
  • Add end locations to scalars and aliases in (#16187)
  • Set JSON::SerializableError#attribute when appropriate (#16158)
  • Add parameter to (#16232)
  • Add parameter to (#16208)
  • Support 0X, 0O, 0B prefixes in string to integer conversion (#16226)

The memory management model for libxml2 changed in Crystal 1.17 to become manual instead of integrating the GC. The change introduced some memory leaks that have now been fixed.

  • Memory leak in and methods (#16414)
  • Memory leak in (#16418)
  • Memory leak in (#16419)
  • Fix use after unlink in (#16432)
  • All overloads of return on out of bounds (#16453)

The compiler is now always built with execution contexts, and the LLVM codegen is now always multithreaded for every target, including Windows. We can finally wave goodbye to fork, and Windows should see a performance boost.

  • Build linux aarch64 tarballs (#16330)
  • Build snap arm64 target (#16491)
  • Enable multiarch docker builds (#16493)
  • Encourage +1 reactions on issues and PRs for prioritization (#16241)

The OpenSSL bindings now require OpenSSL 1.1.1+ or LibreSSL 3+. We expect the change to not have any effect since these versions are no longer distributed by any supported operating system.

  • Deprecate single-letter macro fresh variables with indices (#16267)
  • Deprecate macro fresh variables with constant names (#16293)
  • Deprecate StringLiteral#split(ASTNode) (#16439)
  • Deprecate (#16545)
  • Deprecate Time#inspect(io, *, with_nanoseconds) (#16416)

We have been able to do all of this thanks to the continued support of 84codes and every other sponsor. To maintain and increase the development pace, donations and sponsorships are essential. OpenCollective is available for that.

Reach out to crystal@manas.tech if you’d like to become a direct sponsor or find other ways to support Crystal. We thank you in advance!

Contribute

items.title: Show HN: Crystal Unified – Search compressed archives without decompressing
items.author: danebl
items.content:
Crystal Unified lets you grep through compressed archives (700MB logs, etc.) in milliseconds without extracting.

Other features: - Binary delta patching for firmware/OTA updates - Block-level random access - 10x+ compression on logs - Genomic data: 4:1 on 2-bit encoded DNA, 111,000:1 with reference compression - Parallel compression, 1GB/s+ decompression
